speaker: interviewer
text: Born, Jukin. Just before the break, you said you were going to tell us the spelling of the name of the Polish lady that hid you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I see you're still in touch with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. J-A-N-I-C-K-A. That's her-- that's her-- that's her--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last name, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-C-H-A-L-I-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that Michalina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michalina. Michalina. You will say, 'Michelle-ina.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Michelle, like Michelle.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's a C-H. And I have her address, naturally. I have her picture here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she live in the same place that she did when you were hiding? Her address, is that the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. That's not-- no, no. She's not there anymore. No. They burned the whole-- the whole village, the Ukrainians, I told you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you-- you escaped the ghetto, with a few other people, that was before it was destroyed or completely liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I assume, yes. Everything was liquidated. There were no other-- no Jewish people left in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get any news about it from anybody, or-- you just ran away and that was the last you heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just, right. I just find out a lot about them in this book.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have a book about the history of-- of your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That put together in Israel. My friend-- my friend send me the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the author of the book? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no-- This is in Hebrew. Maybe you want to read it?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a book about your town, Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: About my town. All the pictures, and all the people I know, everybody, everything of the size of the ghetto, the size of town, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it says--

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: [HEBREW] It looked like [HEBREW] and the-- the-- [HEBREW] one who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wrote the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one who organized it is [INAUDIBLE]. That's probably his Hebrew name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who sent to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend, Mikhal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Mikhal sent it to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it tells [INTERPOSING VOICES] all about the life in Stepan? OK. We're going to look at pictures later on from that-- from that book. So you were in hiding, and you kept going from one Polish family to Another

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where you wanted to go? Did you have a goal? You just wanted to stay alive and just go from one house to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. No idea where I'm going, if I'm going to get there. I mean the four of us, that's not only me. Then we separate with the boys too, at the end. But it's going to-- this is some days--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you left, did you take anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: From where?

speaker: interviewer
text: From the ghetto. What did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just whatever I had on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got food from the people that you hid with? Did you-- how did you find a hiding place every night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost, if not we slept in the fields outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about food then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were still going around for-- begging for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So tell us what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: We never had a bath. We never had-- washed our face. We never had nothing. Only with water from--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the bathroom, you just went in the fields?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything in the field. We had no home. We were homeless.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody ever took you, like, inside their home for a little while to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Yeah, I told you, the Polish in the wintertime, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, when you told me, it was before in the pre-interview, but we want to hear it now on this tape like it's new. OK? So whatever you tell me, is I'd like it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I told you before. That when it came-- cold weather, the-- my Polish family took me in, and not only me, sometimes-- sometimes with my friend, girlfriend, but not really the boys. And so they--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would they do? Say, we don't want the boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boys-- the boys was a problem. Because there was-- they were Jewish-looking. They were-- I didn't look Jewish, let's say. I don't know. But the boys was a little problem that they didn't want the boys in the house. It was-- they had two kids of their own. They had two children. But the older son is-- was a year younger than I. And the younger one was about two or three years older-- younger than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at that point about?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- when we go into forest, 14 was almost also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well the problem with the boys is probably because the Germans could tell if the boys had a circumcision. They knew that they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's exactly what I was going to say, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but you didn't stay in this one house all the time? You keep saying your Polish family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because with them, they-- they really, thanks to them I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. While-- while were were in the village, we were-- we were going around, you know. But what happened during the wintertime, the Polish people were fighting with the Ukrainians. The reason why-- I-- I-- actually it's the other way around. The-- the Ukrainian people always wanted to have their own Ukraine. And they never really-- never could really get it. So since-- so the German people told the soldiers, they told them, if you help since we got rid of the Jews now, if you'll help us get rid of the Polacks, then it's gonna be your Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did it. So what-- so what happened, they started to fight and burn down all the houses from in the village, from the Polish-- the Polish houses. So Polacks knew how to fight too, the Polish people. So that they did, they gathered in one-- they gathered all in one place, in one house, two houses. And they were fighting from that area. There weren't-- there was no bombs or swords, but they're all out with a pistol or so. But they were fighting. The Ukrainian would attack the Polish homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called-- that time that was the name what the fighters, Ukrainian fighters, was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the name that was given to them, like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They're called the [NON-ENGLISH], yeah. And so finally, they were fighting for a couple of days, like eight days or so. I was with this Polish family hiding in the same area. So were the other girl, but on her own. But the boys, I didn't see. Because they were already, I think, they-- they teamed up at that time already, I think, with the partisans. Because there were also partisans in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish partisans or Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Jewish or non-Jewish. I was not there. Because my Polish lady said to me, you can't go to partisans. Because you're a girl. You're a young girl. You know what partisans can do to-- to girls. So you go with me. Where we go, you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had to escape also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So what happened, we all had to escape. And we escaped to another town, to a bigger town that's like a-- was like a-- like--

speaker: interviewer
text: A resort?

speaker: interviewee
text: A resort sort of like. It was like they had the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spas, health? What was the name of the town, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. I forgot. So, she said, you come with us. So I said, so I went with them. And then we stayed there not too long. We all had no homes anymore. The Polacks--

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't either?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Her house was burned. Everything was burned. So, and there was no work in those families. So we-- they decided to go-- the German people at that time, needed people to come to Germany to work on the farms and things. Because most of the people, all the young people were in the army. So they decided to go to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said to me, you coming with us. I said, come on. The minute I'll see a German soldier, they'll recognize I'm Jewish. And what's gonna be with you? Never mind me, but what's going to be with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said to me, don't worry. And I went with her, with them. And I went with them. She said, we're not going to leave you here. And we came to Germany. We had to go through a inspection, you know, the doctor inspection. You know, they checked us if we're healthy or not. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a false name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She gave me her maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was-- it was-- Henya [? Shalakowska. ?] [? Geneveva ?] [? Shalakowska. ?] So we gave the name. And she told me that if-- I should say that she-- I'm her cousin. And I lost my-- I was an only child. And I have-- lost my-- during the war, I lost-- my father died and my-- my mother died during the war. And I have nobody, except them. And that was-- that's when we got papers that made out my passport, or whatever. They never-- they never gave us any papers. Because this-- then a day later, after this, a day later, the farmers came. And they were trying to pick people to-- for their farm, to work, to live with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you're talking about Polish farmers or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: By now you're in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But now, Scheidemuhl-- did I say Scheidemuhl?

speaker: interviewer
text: No you didn't say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Schneidemuhl.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a town in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But didn't you-- wasn't that a very long trip? Because you lived on the eastern part of-- of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we went-- with-- with the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about two, three days or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there-- were there gestapo on the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody, nobody stopped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. it was only Polish people. There were no Jews, except me. But they don't know. I'm not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But nobody was looking for any Jews there on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not there, not on the train, nothing, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were by yourself? You weren't with the other girl anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, I saw the other girl before we started-- before we went in, depart to Germany. We had a stop in Rovno, that city.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, and they stayed there a few days or so. And they took us to work, the young people only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took you to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess the Germans or the Poles, I don't really know. And then we-- they put us on a truck, regular trucks. And then I got on the truck, I saw this girl sitting. But we never exchanged a word with each other. As a matter of fact, there was another one from my town sitting, older than me. Never said one word, like we never knew each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: For safety reasons, because if not, if I would start talking-- I mean, our Polish was good. But we still had a slight accent. But we couldn't-- we couldn't open our mouth-- my mouth, you know? Or she couldn't open her mouth too. And we-- and we were afraid they should catch on. So we didn't speak to each other, and the same thing when they took us back. We didn't-- they dropped us off. They dropped everybody who was picked up from-- from, you know, were dropped off at the same spot. So we went to-- to they dropped me off where they picked me up, to where the Polish family stayed. And that was only like a couple days. And then from there, we went to Schneidemuhl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went with the Polish family--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with the Polish family--

speaker: interviewer
text: To Schneidemuhl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schneidemuhl by train, and then when we get there, the same thing. They started to-- they-- we went through this, like I said before, we went through the doctors and this or checkup. So they checked us out, if we're healthy. And because if you're not healthy, they really didn't want you there. They want people to-- to go to work. And what-- well, the next morning, they told us to line up, who are all the newcomers. And the people came from the-- the-- the farmers. And they were picking up families. And my, the family, was a healthy-- healthy young people that-- the parents was still very young. They had the children, two children, that they were able-- that could, the older son could work. I could work. So they took us immediately, and they send us to this gutsverwaltung. That was a huge farm. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gutsverwaltung?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was in German it was gutsverwaltung.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that G-U-T-V-E-R-A-L-T-U-N-G?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gutsverwaltung.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gutsverwaltung, and-- and that was managed by German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well he was, but he stayed there-- everyone, I think, was supposed-- was supposed to-- had to be a Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember anybody's name of the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- you know, I have the picture, you know. Anyway, so they took us to the gutsverwaltung. This was located between-- in a small area town, between Deutsch Krone, which was-- well let's say, and Schneidemuhl. Schneidemuhl was the biggest city, and Deutsch Krone was a smaller town. It was closer to the farm too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Deutsch Krone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deutsch Krone, yeah. And but we stayed-- the place where we stayed was-- was-- [INAUDIBLE]. I forgot already. It was a name that the farm, you know-- Buchenwald? No, not Buchenwald. I forgot. Maybe it will come to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a town where you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, beginning I did what all, everybody did. Like the Polish people, they were also war prisoners there, Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody worked on the farm, in the field, everybody, even Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you harvesting or weeding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harvesting, weeding, whatever had to be-- pick potatoes, whatever it was we had to do. And then the-- even the Germans that were still at home, the kids, they had to work too. And then-- then they took all these children, the young-- young men and women to the army. At the end of the war was this already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh yeah, what year was this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The end-- the end of war, we liberated '45, yeah, maybe '46, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the story that you told me took like four or five years that you were with that family all the time? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: From starting from Wirke till-- from the Poland, it was probably was like four or five years. Then we lived together after the war a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember like how, when time passed, did you-- were they-- did they celebrate their holidays? Did they have Christmases? Was there anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we did have Christmas naturally. And I celebrate their holidays. I did not celebrate my holidays. Because then I wouldn't be-- then I became Polish. As soon as we checked into Scheidemuhl-- my name was not-- it was no more-- nobody knew I'm Jewish, except my family. I was Polish. And I-- that's why I had my name, [? Shalakowska, ?] and all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henya.

speaker: interviewee
text: Henya.

speaker: interviewer
text: H-E-N-Y-A?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you feel when you were celebrating the holidays with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt very normal, very-- very normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think about your Jewish holidays and your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home and by myself. And-- and-- when I go-- went to sleep or so. But nothing, I had Polish friends. Even a boyfriend, Polish. I got a little older. Everybody, we were-- Polish family was like one Polish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You really felt, did you feel like you were Polish, or you would just--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I started to feel I'm Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you would have been ready to give up your Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is another story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we were liberated, I said to my Polish lady, we went back to-- first we ran away. Because we-- when we're-- we were liberated by the Russian army. When the Russians were coming closer to us, we had to-- they made us to go further.

speaker: interviewer
text: Further where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Away from-- away from town. And we stopped somewhere on a-- in a different area.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Germany at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now the Russians were fighting against the Germans by now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Against the Germans, yeah. So when they came and we had-- we went-- we went further, further where the Germans were still occupied-- which was still occupied by Germans. Then we stopped. And that's where-- and then I forgot about about [INAUDIBLE], where we were liberated. Anyway, now-- then after liberation, also we had which I didn't tell you. At the end-- when-- I didn't tell you the story that I came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: After them-- they started to-- all-- all the German youngsters went to the army. She lost her maid, see, the lady of the house. So from all the people--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean another lady of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the German, now I'm talking the German. She lost her maid, her help. So she picked me to be the maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she see you? How did she know you? What was your relationship with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She know from that I'm from the Polish family. I mean we worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you worked at her house anyway?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At the field-- on the field.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: In her field?

speaker: interviewee
text: In her field, yeah. However she managed the [NON-ENGLISH]. So-- so she decided to take me in. And I was-- I was sleeping there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your Polish family at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next door, next house. They was-- they were there. They still worked on the farm. But I worked-- I think the reason she took me in is I used to speak Yiddish. And don't forget, Yiddish and German is very close. So I learned how to speak the German. The Polish people could never-- the youngsters learned a little bit, but not the middle-aged or so could not speak any other language. German, no. They spoke a little, maybe Ukrainian, because it's also very close. So-- so I was-- I was translating the-- now let's say all the mail from all the people, the Ukrainian, Polish, came into the main house where I worked. And he, the man came, and everything came into this office. And he told me which-- this letter came to this one. This one came to them, or vice versa. They came in for the letters. And everything that had to be, they wanted to know the Polish people or the Ukrainian people, they had to use me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your German boss?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], no. [INAUDIBLE], yeah Wait, [INAUDIBLE], ooh. I cannot remember. I have to think again. Because I had a friend, [INAUDIBLE], I might mix them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a Nazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very nice guy, very nice. At the end-- he-- I can say he had to go to the army too. It was to the last-- maybe-- the Russians was already coming. They took him. He-- he wore a-- I saw him in a uniform, not all the time, but specially when they used to have friends, invited the Nazi friends to their house for dinner, holidays. I served them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you even hear any talk about the war or about their attitudes about Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not allowed to hear. I didn't know anything about the war. I didn't know if there are any Jewish people left. After the war, I thought I'm the only one who survived. And then I was thinking, what will I do? To be only one survivor of Jewish survivor in the whole Europe, Europe. But thank god, that more people survived. So-- so this was a day even he left to the army. Then she was left with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was his wife, yeah. And she said to me-- they not-- had no children. And she said to me, you know, I would like to adopt you as my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had no idea you're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea, not to my--

speaker: interviewer
text: But she knew that you were part of the other family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I came with the family, and I'm the cousin. I said, no. Sorry. I came with my family. I got to go back with my family. Wherever we go, I want to be with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she's left-- Around the same-- it's only months of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but this is years after you left the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like 14--

speaker: interviewer
text: This isn't years after you left the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: This wasn't years after you left--

speaker: interviewee
text: After, yeah, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by now you must be 18 or 19.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, about 15. No, when I-- 18, I was not quite 18 when I was-- after the war, when the war was over. I married my husband when I was like-- I was [INAUDIBLE]. I was-- I needed somebody to sign for me. I was still not fully-- like a minor. Anyway, where was I?

speaker: interviewer
text: So this German lady wanted to adopt you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, yeah. Well, she was left. And the-- the Russian army was coming near closer, nearer and nearer to us. So she came from Berlin. And she want to-- she packed everything up. And she was going to Berlin. And that's when she wanted me to with her. And she-- she wanted me to be-- she wanted me to be her daughter. And that's when I said, no. And we, also all the Polacks and Russian, we-- we had to also go further up, run away from the-- from the Russian army, until there was a stop, finished. And that's where I was-- we were liberated, in [INAUDIBLE], or something like this, a town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that near? Do you know? [SIGHS] That's in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany, I'm talking about Germany now. I'm not talking about Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, your Polish family was also fleeing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody had to. Because they didn't-- they didn't want us to be-- to be liberated with the-- with the Russians. They did-- I assume they probably thought, the German people, that they're not losing the war. That this is gonna come back, you know, it's just temporarily. But then it-- we know in the end what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then-- so I said, no. And she left by herself. And I went with my family. And that's where we-- we was-- it was [NON-ENGLISH], or so, the name of the town. We stopped there for another couple of weeks or so, maybe less. And then the Russians came, and we were liberated. So then the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in May?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: In April, 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Then we were-- I went back to the Polish family, back to the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the lady's farm in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, who was occupied already by the Russian soldiers. And we was-- stayed there for a while. And then we moved to another farm, which was close by. It was a smaller farm. And finally I started to see-- and there was-- there was at that time, was the Polish family and me, and the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the whole family stayed together, though? Nothing happened to any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing happened to any of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The husband was with you also?

speaker: interviewee
text: But during the time when we were traveling, I don't remember-- and we were going also-- I don't know how we come-- we went through Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the husband with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whose husband?

speaker: interviewer
text: With the German-- the Polish family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. When we were going, by the time-- I don't know how this can be-- back to the farm-- we went to the Scheidemuhl. I know we went through the Berlin, and we saw Berlin on fire. And we saw-- we went through the woods, wooded area, we also-- I think it was Berlin. And it was also a lot, a lot of, killed there from the soldiers. We was almost walking on the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the German Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the German Army and the Russian Army, both, because they were fighting for every-- every step. So it took a long-- took a while until-- until we were liberated, until we got to get liberated. But then after, when we were liberated, we went back to the-- to the farm. And then finally, after a while, they were all Russian, occupied Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But wait-- do you mind if I stop you a second? When you will liberated, like what actually happened? What were you doing? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the minute we were liberated, we were still in-- we also were hiding in the forest because of these airplanes that, you know, are shooting and things like that. I said, oh my god. I'm liberated? And what about my family?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know you were liberated if you're in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was because the Russian soldiers came in, and they came to start to speak Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said the war is over?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, the war is over. The war is over. So we came out from the-- forest. And I said, oh my god, oh myself. What about my family? Did they survive or they didn't? I didn't know anything about it. And where will I find Jewish people? I mean, here where I am now, there's no Jewish people. So-- but anyway, that's all-- that was the first thing that came in my mind. And then we were moved back to-- we went-- I went-- wherever the family went, I went. I was with them. And then finally, they left. I said to them, you know, I'd like to go to the cities, Polish bigger cities. And look, maybe I can find, maybe if there are any Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they weren't going back to Poland? Your family was staying in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was like, from-- since Germany-- Scheidemuhl became Poland. After the Russian occupation, they gave it to the Polacks. It became Polish. Because the area where I came from, when I-- I was born, was the Russian. Was no more Polish Ukraine. It was Russian Ukraine or whatever. So they gave them the Scheidemuhl that they named it Pila. Schneidemuhl is like, is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tailor.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not-- no you cut something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Pila is also, you-- it's like when you when you have-- you will have this-- what do you call-- you cut, cut. You cut, you cut wood or sawing you,

speaker: interviewer
text: A saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Pila is the Polish name for Schneidemuhl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right, right. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We have to cut now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't-- That was a long story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll finish after-- after the break, we'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I hope it's going to-- am finishing already.
